title: Boldness
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.title: Introduction: The Vav Section of Psalm 119
subpoints: The Vav section (vv. 41-48) is structurally unique: the letter vav also means 'and,' so every verse in Hebrew begins with 'and'—the most connecting of the 22 stanzas
Grammatically, each two verses form one sentence rather than one verse per sentence as in previous sections
The question posed: who here has lacked boldness to share the gospel, felt ashamed of the Christian position, or would like to grow in boldness for the Lord?
This section is a prayer for boldness (vv. 41-43) followed by a commitment to boldness (vv. 44-48)

outline.title: A Prayer for Boldness: Anchored in the Mercies of God (v. 41)
subpoints: 'Let Your mercies come also to me, O LORD, Your salvation according to Your word'—boldness finds its anchor in the mercies of God
The word for 'mercies' here is hesed—God's covenantal faithfulness, His disposition toward us because He has entered into covenant and will never deny His name
Great is God's faithfulness not because of us (we are changeable) but because of His own name and promises, which He will never deny
This prayer in verse 41 is the only way the rest of the passage could be possible—everything rests on God's mercies and salvation

outline.title: Boldness to Answer the Reproaches of Unbelievers (v. 42)
subpoints: 'So shall I have an answer for him who reproaches me, for I trust in Your word'—to answer unbelievers, the believer's confidence is not in his own words but in the Word of God
The psalmist prays that God will give him words from God's Word to answer the reproaches; we do not try to answer scoffing with our own wisdom but with what God has said
Every believer knows the gospel and can articulate it—if you don't know how to express the gospel, that is a serious concern worth examining
We give a reason for the hope that lies within us (1 Peter 3:15) not in terms of what we personally think but in terms of 'thus says the Lord'

outline.title: The Psalmist's Greatest Fear: Not Speaking the Word (v. 43)
subpoints: 'Do not take the word of truth utterly out of my mouth, for I have hoped in Your ordinances'—the psalmist's greatest fear is being kept from speaking God's Word, not speaking it
We are often afraid of the slightest persecution; the godly person's fear should be not speaking God's truth even under the greatest persecution
The godly fear God's disapproval, not men's disapproval—that is seen in this prayer: he prays that his mouth will never stop declaring God's truth no matter the cost
This is a prayer for grace and the boldness to believe and to speak God's truth—we must pray earnestly for this

outline.title: A Commitment to Boldness: Obeying the Word Continually (v. 44)
subpoints: 'So shall I keep Your law continually, forever and ever'—a commitment to obey God's Word as the eternal activity of the Christian
Obedience here is training for heaven: in Revelation 22 John describes the eternal state and says 'His servants shall serve Him'—we will be obeying and serving God forever
This pattern of prayer followed by commitment shows the psalmist never content merely to pray but always answers his own prayers with action
This commitment is made in the context of opposition—the same opposition that prompted the prayer for boldness

outline.title: Walking at Liberty in the Wide Space (v. 45)
subpoints: 'And I will walk at liberty, for I seek Your precepts'—to walk at liberty literally means to walk in a wide space, in safety, without fear of falling off the edges
The psalmist finds safety in following God's Word; we often think of God's Word as restrictive, but we are designed to follow it and are freest when we do
Walking in the wide space means being free from worry about what is around us, so we can focus on what God calls us to do
Liberty is in following God's Word; enslavement is in not following it—we walk unshackled as we follow the Lord

outline.title: Speaking Before Kings and the History of Boldness (v. 46)
subpoints: 'I will speak of Your testimonies also before kings, and will not be ashamed'—echoed by Jesus (Matthew 10) and Paul's declaration of not being ashamed of the gospel (Romans 1:16)
Polycarp at 86 years old: 'Eighty-six years have I served Him, and He has never done me injury—how then can I now blaspheme my King and Savior?'—burned at the stake
Justin Martyr to Emperor Marcus Aurelius: 'You can kill us, but you cannot hurt us'
John Hus going to his execution: 'He fears death who loses the joy of life... Truth conquers all things'—ordinary people like you and me, empowered by the same grace available today

outline.title: Boldness Flows from Conviction and Love (vv. 47-48)
subpoints: 'I will delight myself in Your commandments, which I love'—the psalmist could respond to his own prayer with boldness because he was convicted that the Bible is true and he loved it
Embarrassment and shame in standing for the Christian faith may come from unbelief—we may be embarrassed of the Christian position because we don't really believe it
You cannot love what you don't believe; yet the psalmist loved the Word of God and was convinced of its truth, and therefore could stand in boldness
Application: Do you lack boldness? Ask the Lord for it as the psalmist did—then practice the boldness God has promised to give you

summary:
This Sunday school sermon works through Psalm 119:41-48, the Vav section, which Pastor Tito Lyro notes is structurally unique—every verse begins with the conjunction 'and' in Hebrew, and grammatically each pair of verses forms a single sentence. He opens with three pointed questions: Who has lacked boldness to share the gospel? Who has felt ashamed of the Christian position? Who would like to grow in boldness for the Lord?

The passage divides into two movements: a prayer for boldness (vv. 41-43) and a commitment to boldness (vv. 44-48). The prayer for boldness is anchored in God's hesed—His covenantal faithfulness and mercy—which is great not because of us but because of His own name. From that foundation the psalmist prays for words from God's Word to answer those who reproach him, and most strikingly, his greatest fear is not speaking God's truth but being kept from speaking it. Unlike us who fear even mild disapproval, the psalmist fears God's disapproval above all.

The commitment section shows the pattern characteristic of this psalmist: he not only prays but acts. He commits to obeying God's Word continually—which is training for heaven, where His servants serve Him forever. He describes walking 'at liberty' in a wide space—a metaphor for the safety and freedom that comes from following God's Word, contrasting with the popular notion that God's commands are restrictive. The sermon reaches its climax with historical examples of ordinary believers who spoke before kings with extraordinary boldness: Polycarp, Justin Martyr, and John Hus. The closing point is that boldness flows from conviction and love—embarrassment about the Christian faith betrays unbelief, while love for the Word that one truly believes produces natural, joyful boldness.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Psalm 119:41-48

Let Your mercies come also to me, O LORD—Your salvation according to Your word. So shall I have an answer for him who reproaches me, for I trust in Your word. And take not the word of truth utterly out of my mouth, for I have hoped in Your ordinances. So shall I keep Your law continually, forever and ever. And I will walk at liberty, for I seek Your precepts. I will speak of Your testimonies also before kings, and will not be ashamed. And I will delight myself in Your commandments, which I love. My hands also I will lift up to Your commandments, which I love, and I will meditate on Your statutes.

## Sermon

### Introduction: The Vav Section

We are coming to the Vav section of Psalm 119, verses 41 through 48. This is structurally the most connecting of the 22 sections. The letter vav is also the word 'and'—a conjunction. So every verse in this section in Hebrew starts with the word 'and.' As far as the Hebrew poetic structure goes, the structure is the most straightforward of the 22 sessions—though the content is not boring at all. Also, this section is grammatically different from the previous ones: each two verses form a sentence, not each single verse, as in the sections that came before.

Let me ask you this: who here has lacked boldness to share the gospel with unbelievers? I'll raise my hand. Who at times has felt ashamed or embarrassed of the Christian position in a particular issue—where everyone around you holds one view and you know the Christian belief is another, and there's a twinge of embarrassment about it? Who here would like to grow in boldness for the Lord and the gospel? I hope everybody—raise both hands.

This section of Psalm 119 is a prayer for boldness to stand on and for the Word of God amid opposition. It divides into two sections: verses 41 through 43, a prayer for boldness; and verses 44 through 48, a commitment to boldness. The psalmist prays and then puts feet to his prayers—he is never content with just praying; he also answers his prayers with action.

### Part 1: A Prayer for Boldness (vv. 41-43)

**Boldness Anchored in the Mercies of God (v. 41)**

'Let Your mercies come also to me, O LORD, Your salvation according to Your word.'

Boldness finds its anchor in the mercies of God. The word for 'mercies' here is hesed—sometimes translated loving-kindness or steadfast love. This is a certain disposition of God toward us because He has entered into covenant and His name is staked upon His promises. He will never deny them—not because of us, because we change and go from here to there and everywhere, but because of His own name and His own promises. That is why His mercy is the anchor to boldness: according to His mercy, He is going to give us all things that we need to stand for the gospel.

Remember the hymn: 'Great Is Thy Faithfulness.' The refrain says 'unto me'—and there is truth to that. But God's faithfulness is great not because of us but because of His name's sake. He is never going to deny it. And that's why His mercy is inexhaustible.

The prayer in verse 41 is the only way the rest of the passage could be possible. Everything rests on God's mercies and salvation.

**Boldness to Answer Reproaches (v. 42)**

'So shall I have an answer for him who reproaches me, for I trust in Your word.'

These are the taunting opposition and criticisms aimed against those who trust in Scripture. To answer unbelievers, the believer's confidence is not in his own words but in the words of God. The psalmist prays that God will give him words from God's Word to answer the reproaches. We do not try to answer reproach or mocking or scoffing by our own wisdom—we answer by the words of God.

We are to be ready to give an answer—that is what Peter tells us in 1 Peter 3:15: be ready to give an answer to anyone who asks why you hope. And this is not just for pastors, elders, and teachers. Every true believer knows the gospel, because you cannot be a believer without knowing the gospel. Our faith is objective—based on a particular set of facts. If you don't know how to articulate the gospel, that is worth examining carefully.

When we give a reason for the hope within us, we do it not in terms of what we personally think but in terms of 'thus says the Lord': this is what God says about Himself; this is what God says about me; this is what God says about the Lord Jesus Christ.

**The Psalmist's Greatest Fear: Not Speaking (v. 43)**

'And take not the word of truth utterly out of my mouth, for I have hoped in Your ordinances.'

We may have a great fear of speaking God's Word in public. But the psalmist's greatest fear was not speaking the Word in public—it was being kept from speaking it. He prays that his mouth will never stop declaring God's truth no matter the situation, no matter the cost.

We are often afraid of speaking God's truth when there is the slightest persecution—somebody looks at us funny or says something that suggests disagreement. Yet the godly person's fear should be not speaking God's truth even in the greatest persecution. The godly fear God's disapproval, not men's disapproval.

In Acts 4, when the first serious persecution came and some of the apostles were jailed, after they were released they came back to the other disciples and rejoiced in the honor of suffering for the sake of the gospel. Then they prayed—not that God would remove persecution, but that God would give them boldness to keep on proclaiming the very word that had gotten them thrown in jail. And God answered by giving them the very boldness that landed them in jail again.

### Part 2: A Commitment to Boldness (vv. 44-48)

**Obeying the Word Continually, Forever (v. 44)**

'So shall I keep Your law continually, forever and ever.'

This is the commitment to obey God's Word as the eternal activity of the Christian. We are going to be obeying God's Word forever. In Revelation 22:3, John describes the eternal state: 'the throne of God and of the Lamb shall be in it, and His servants shall serve Him'—we will be obeying and serving God in heaven, perfectly and joyfully. The psalmist realized that obedience here is training for heaven.

**Walking at Liberty in the Wide Space (v. 45)**

'And I will walk at liberty, for I seek Your precepts.'

To walk at liberty literally means to walk in a wide space—to walk in safety, knowing you are not going to fall off the edges. On a narrow mountain trail, you can trip and fall. In a wide space, you walk at liberty. The psalmist finds safety in following God's Word; he doesn't have to worry about what is going to happen to him. He can focus entirely on what God calls him to do—because God is going to take care of the rest, even if He calls him home through death.

We often think of God's Word as restrictive, keeping us from doing all the things. But that is not true. We are designed to follow God's Word, and we are at our best—at our freest—when we are following it. Liberty is in following God's Word. Enslavement is in not following it. We walk unshackled as we follow the Lord.

As Martin Luther wrote in 'A Mighty Fortress'—not from the comfort of his living room, but on the back of a wagon going to the Diet of Worms where he was sure he would die: 'And though this world with devils filled should threaten to undo us, we will not fear, for God hath willed His truth to triumph through us... That word above all earthly powers, no thanks to them, abideth.' That is walking in a wide space. That is walking at liberty. Luther was going to his death, and he was assured that he was in a safe place because it was the Word of God he was going to proclaim.

**Speaking Before Kings (v. 46)**

'I will speak of Your testimonies also before kings, and will not be ashamed.'

This echoes what Jesus says when He sends out the disciples—you are going to proclaim the Word of God to kings, but don't worry about it; the Spirit will bring to mind what you should say. And it echoes Paul in Romans 1:16: 'I am not ashamed of the gospel, for it is the power of God to salvation.'

The history of the church is filled with examples of this boldness. Polycarp, born in the first century, a disciple of the Apostle John through Papias: at 86 years old he is imprisoned and brought before the magistrate who says, 'Old man, all you have to do is deny Christ—just say the words and you'll be okay.' Polycarp says: 'Eighty-six years have I served Him, and He has never done me injury. How then can I now blaspheme my King and Savior?' He was burned at the stake at 86 years of age.

Justin Martyr declared to Emperor Marcus Aurelius: 'You can kill us, but you cannot hurt us.'

John Hus, as he was brought before the magistrate who threatened to break his head, said: 'He fears death who loses the joy of life. Truth conquers all things—he conquers who dies for it. No adversity will harm him if he is not dominated by iniquity.' He told them several truths and was killed for them.

These were people just like you and me—not super-Christians, not special people. They lived in a world marred with sin, just as we do. They boldly stood for Christ and His Word because the grace of God—the same grace that is available to us today—empowered them to do so.

**Boldness from Conviction and Love (vv. 47-48)**

'And I will delight myself in Your commandments, which I love. My hands also I will lift up to Your commandments, which I love, and I will meditate on Your statutes.'

The psalmist was able to respond to his own prayer with boldness because he was convicted that the Bible is true—and he loved it. Embarrassment and shame in standing for the Christian faith may come from a place of unbelief. We may be embarrassed of the Christian position because we don't really believe it. You cannot love what you don't believe. Yet this psalmist loved the Word of God, was convinced of its truth, and because of that could stand in boldness and speak the Word of God.

So let me ask you as we close: Do you lack boldness? Ask the Lord for it—that is what the psalmist did. Pray earnestly for it. And then, once you've prayed, practice the boldness that God has promised to give you.
